cue: How do you say someone is filling your head with nonsense?

es.literal: to fill the head with little birds
es.meaning: To fill someone's head with empty promises or unrealistic talk.
es.example: No le llenes la cabeza de pajaritos, que luego se ilusiona.
es.exampleTranslation: Don't fill her head with nonsense — she'll get her hopes up.

it.text: Mettere grilli in testa a qualcuno
it.literal: to put crickets in someone's head
it.meaning: To put unrealistic ideas into someone's head.
it.example: Non mettergli grilli in testa, poi ci spera davvero.
it.exampleTranslation: Don't put ideas in his head — he'll actually count on it.

bridge.note: The causative twin of entry 0074, one grammatical step over: there the head already HAS the creatures, here somebody PUTS them in. Both languages carry both forms with the same animals — Spanish birds, Italian crickets — which means the pair teaches a whole grammatical family rather than a single phrase. And the blame shifts with the verb: having birds makes you a dreamer, being given them makes you a mark.

interference.es_to_it: Birds again fail in Italian: the head takes grilli, and the verb is mettere IN testa (a qualcuno), not riempire.
interference.it_to_es: 'Poner grillos en la cabeza' is not Spanish; fill it with little birds — and note the diminutive, pajaritos, which carries the condescension.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh here). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Stative/causative sibling of 0074 — the clearest case yet for a themed two-day sequence.
